column1: #mapping_set_description: Mapping from OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 to IBM AI Risk Atlas, Granite Guardian and AIUC-1

column9: comment
Both involve injection attacks that manipulate system behavior; agent goal hijack extends to multi-step workflows
Both involve circumventing intended constraints through indirect manipulation
Priming can set the context for goal redirection through subtle instruction influence
Tool selection and execution can be manipulated via prompt injection
Misused tools can lead to unauthorized data access and misuse
Over-reliance on agent tool recommendations can lead to unsafe tool application
Identity abuse directly enables unauthorized access through privilege escalation
Both involve misuse of identity for unintended purposes
Privilege abuse can lead to exposure of protected information
Compromised components (tools, schemas) contaminate the agent's knowledge and decision-making
Compromised tool descriptions lead to misleading information and advice
Supply chain compromise poisons the data and schemas agents depend on
Jailbreaking can enable exploitation of code generation to execute arbitrary code
Prompt injection can manipulate code generation and execution
Both involve exploiting system capabilities to gain unauthorized access to code or internals
Memory and context are forms of data that can be poisoned to corrupt agent behavior
Poisoned context can increase hallucination risk as agents reason over contaminated information
Biased or misleading data in memory causes biased agent behavior
Agent messages can be injected with malicious content (agent-in-the-middle variant of prompt injection)
Inter-agent messages can leak attributes and sensitive information through side-channels
Unencrypted or unauthenticated communication violates data privacy principles
Hallucinations can propagate across agents, amplifying errors
Contaminated data spreads through agent networks, causing cascading failures
Accuracy failures in one agent propagate through the system as cascades
Over-reliance on agents is the mechanism through which trust exploitation occurs
Agents can provide misleading advice that humans trust and act upon
Opaque agent reasoning makes it harder for humans to verify trustworthiness
Rogue agents may make incorrect function calls leading to unintended actions
Rogue agents deviate from intended use and authorized scope
Rogue behavior emerges from complex agent interactions and requires ongoing oversight
Jailbreak attempts can be used to manipulate agent goals and circumvent safeguards
General harmful intent in prompts that could redirect agent objectives
Detects unsafe or misaligned function calls in agent systems
Jailbreaking attempts bypass tool safety constraints
Detects harmful patterns in prompts attempting privilege escalation
Detects malicious content in dynamically discovered tools and schemas
Detects evasive or misleading descriptions in supply chain components
Jailbreak attempts could lead to code generation and execution exploits
Detects unsafe or adversarial function/code execution sequences
Detects ungrounded responses indicating poisoned context or corrupted memory
Detects when retrieved or stored context is irrelevant or corrupted
Detects malicious content in inter-agent message exchanges
Detects evasive or spoofed agent communications
Detects hallucinations that could cascade through agent networks
Detects irrelevant or corrupted responses that propagate through agent systems
Detects hallucinations and ungrounded claims that could deceive users
Detects harmful or manipulative agent responses
Jailbreak attempts could cause agent goal drift and behavioral divergence
Detects anomalous or misaligned function calls indicating rogue behavior
Detects harmful intents and behaviors emerging from rogue agents
Prompt injection detection is critical for preventing agent goal hijacking
Direct mapping: unauthorized actions are the manifestation of goal hijack attacks
Core control for preventing tool misuse in agentic systems
Output validation prevents downstream exploitation from misused tools
Tool access should be bounded by principle of least privilege for data
Access controls are fundamental to preventing identity and privilege abuse
Comprehensive audit trails enable detection of privilege escalation patterns
Detailed logging of identity-based agent actions is essential for accountability
Direct mapping: validates upstream tools, schemas, and components
Supply chain governance ensures data integrity through component lifecycle
Prompt injection is a primary vector for code execution attacks
Restricting code execution capabilities is critical control
Validates generated code before execution
Data governance covers data quality and integrity in storage and retrieval
Data lifecycle management includes sanitization and validation of stored data
Logging enables detection of context poisoning through anomaly patterns
Access controls protect inter-agent communication channels
Audit trails of agent communications enable tamper detection
Detailed logging of inter-agent messages enables spoofing detection
Fallbacks directly prevent cascade propagation

column5: Implement circuit breakers for AI agents
column9: Circuit breakers are the primary control for preventing cascading failures
Logging enables early detection of cascading failure patterns
Explainability and documentation enable humans to verify trustworthiness
Audit trails provide transparency for human verification and oversight
Performance monitoring enables detection of degraded accuracy used in deception
Preventing unauthorized actions is the core mitigation for rogue agents

column5: Implement circuit breakers for AI agents
column9: Circuit breakers stop rogue agents from propagating harmful actions
Clear responsibility structures enable rapid identification and isolation of rogue agents
Comprehensive logging enables post-incident forensics on rogue agent behavior
